coredata.dc:description: In this work, a tri-color ratiometric fluorescent optical device integrated with machine learning-assisted smartphones is designed for the visual monitoring of tetracycline antibiotics. The trichromatic sensing probe consists of blue emitting carbon dots (BCDs) at 420 nm, and red-emitting bovine serum albumin-protected copper nanoclusters (BSA-Cu NCs) at 635 nm, as well as an emission peak at 520 nm, rises in the presence of tetracycline antibiotics (TCs), therefore, a three emission system is formed. BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs present three emission responses to TCs, which are based on the internal filtering effect (IFE) and the sensitization mechanisms with BSA. The reaction mechanism was verified by density functional theory (DFT) and a series of characterizations, such as infrared spectrum, ultraviolet absorption spectrum, and fluorescence lifetime. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs was quenched by TCs, and due to the sensitization between BSA and TCs, a green fluorescence rose at 520 nm, while the peak of BSA-Cu NCs remained stable, resulting in a significant color shift from red to cyan. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence intensity of I(635+520)/I420 can be used to determine TCs, and the colors change can be used to identify the quantitative analysis. In addition, the deep learning of the YOLO v3 algorithm assists the integrated tri-color portable optical device of smartphones, which can realize the visual detection of TCs. In general, the sensing system has many advantages, such as good stability, specificity, and rich color changes, which provide help for the visual detection of tetracycline residues.

head.ce:title: Trichromatic ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on machine learning and smartphone for visual and portable monitoring of tetracycline antibiotics

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors wish it to be known that, in their opinions, Zhiwei Lu and Suru Chen should be regarded as joint First Authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a tri-color ratiometric fluorescent optical device integrated with machine learning-assisted smartphones is designed for the visual monitoring of tetracycline antibiotics. The trichromatic sensing probe consists of blue emitting carbon dots (BCDs) at 420 nm, and red-emitting bovine serum albumin-protected copper nanoclusters (BSA-Cu NCs) at 635 nm, as well as an emission peak at 520 nm, rises in the presence of tetracycline antibiotics (TCs), therefore, a three emission system is formed. BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs present three emission responses to TCs, which are based on the internal filtering effect (IFE) and the sensitization mechanisms with BSA. The reaction mechanism was verified by density functional theory (DFT) and a series of characterizations, such as infrared spectrum, ultraviolet absorption spectrum, and fluorescence lifetime. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs was quenched by TCs, and due to the sensitization between BSA and TCs, a green fluorescence rose at 520 nm, while the peak of BSA-Cu NCs remained stable, resulting in a significant color shift from red to cyan. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence intensity of I(635+520)/I420 can be used to determine TCs, and the colors change can be used to identify the quantitative analysis. In addition, the deep learning of the YOLO v3 algorithm assists the integrated tri-color portable optical device of smartphones, which can realize the visual detection of TCs. In general, the sensing system has many advantages, such as good stability, specificity, and rich color changes, which provide help for the visual detection of tetracycline residues.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety is an important issue affecting social development. One of the food safety problems is antibiotic residue. Tetracycline, as a broad-spectrum tetracycline antibiotic, Can reduce the affinity between ribosome and tRNA [1]. Antibiotics are widely used to treat animal diseases, such as bacterial infections [2]. However, the World Health Organization prohibits the use of growth-promoting antibiotics, because the use of antibiotics can lead to various human health problems. When high-concentration antibiotics are used, their residues are found in animal blood and other tissues, which seriously endangers human health [3,4], such as allergy, immunopathological effects, carcinogenicity, nephropathy, hepatotoxicity, and reproductive disorders in humans [5–8]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop detection and elimination technologies for antibiotics to control the risks caused by TCs residues.

Up to now, many technologies have been used to detect TCs [9–12]. However, there are some disadvantages such as expensive instruments and long test time. The fluorescence method has the advantages of fast detection and simple equipment, which can make up for the shortcomings of other methods [13]. Compared with the previous fluorescence detection method, the LOD of our method is lower than some other methods, such as d-His@Au NCs [14], FeOx@SiO2-FMIPs [15], and S-dots/Ca2+ [16]. Although some detection methods have low LOD values, they do not have rich color changes and cannot achieve visual detection of TCs. Our proposed method has rich color changes and can achieve a good visual detection effect. A self-calibration function is added over a single fluorescence signal, which can overcome the interference of uncertain factors such as probe concentration fluctuation, pH value, temperature change, etc [17–19]. Compared with common double emission fluorescence, three emission fluorescence has more abundant color changes. In our previous work, Lu et al. employed three-emission fluorescence to detect copper ions and thiram, which have rich color changes and a wide detection range [20]. Besides, Chen et al. achieved a sensitive detection of Hb in human blood samples by using the three emission fluorescence system and quantitatively analyzed Hb by using rich color variations and RGB analysis [21]. Therefore, three emission signals derived from a ratiometric fluorescence probe can improve the reliability of detection.

Carbon dots (CDs) have excellent fluorescence properties, low toxicity, stability, simple preparation, and other advantages, which have attracted the attention of researchers [22,23]. Due to the unique properties of carbon dots, they are widely used in biological analysis and biological imaging [24,25]. The determination of small molecules and proteins by copper nanoclusters in biological systems has attracted great interest. After coated with bovine serum albumin (BSA) molecular ligand, copper nanoclusters have better biocompatibility and high stability [26].

With the popularity of smartphones, the digital imaging feature makes it to be an outstanding analytical platform to develop point-of-care testing sensors. So far, a lot of biometric detection platform methods using smartphones have been reported [27–35]. However, the stability of image capturing conditions should be considered to ensure the reliability, reproducibility, sensitivity, and accuracy of the smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors. Similarly, differences of other construction components of smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors such as optical filters, power supply facilities, and luminescence efficiency can also lead to color errors in captured images [36]. Besides, the machine learning algorithm can be used to classify target objects [37]. As one of the target detection algorithms, the YOLO v3 can realize the function of simultaneous detection and classification [38]. We developed a WeChat program by ourselves and used the YOLO v3 algorithm to process fluorescence images, which achieved good results. Our group is very interested in the design synthesis of nanohybrid fluorescent probes and has developed CDs-based fluorescent biosensors for monitoring metal ions, pesticides, and small biological molecules in the real sample. Recently, our research group successfully developed a smartphone detection platform based on ratiometric fluorescence to detect Hg2+ and thiram by Au NCs@Fe-MIL-88NH2 probe [39], the monitoring of pesticides by BQDs @G-CdTe@R-CdTe probe [20], and the quantification of H2O2 and glucose by C-dots/Fe2+/VB1 probe and so on [40].

Here, we have developed a portable device that integrates a three-emission fluorescent probe and a smartphone platform. which is used to realize convenient, visual, and portable detection of TCs. In this system, blue-emitting carbon dots and red fluorescent-emitting Cu NCs are used as indicators. In the presence of TCs, due to the internal filtration effect, the blue fluorescence is quenched and the red fluorescence remains unchanged. Due to the interaction between BSA and TCs, the fluorescence peak at 520 nm rises, and with the change of color, the color of the three emission systems changes from red to yellow and then to cyan. A smartphone WeChat applet is developed, which adopts the target detection of YOLO v3 of deep learning (Scheme 1 ). The image is processed by machine learning method, and the color information in the tube is extracted by processing algorithm, which provides good analysis results for TCs detection. Besides, we also compare six different algorithms and choose the best algorithm for fitting. In short, this sensor system has excellent stability and specificity and has rich color changes, which can achieve the purpose of visual detection of TCs.
Reagents, instruments, and material synthesis methods are displayed in the Supporting information.

Different volumes of doxycycline hydrochloride (DC), chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and tetracycline (TC) solution (200 μM), 50 µL BCDs (0.1 g∙L-1) and the solution of BSA- Cu NCs as-prepared were added to the tris-HCL buffer solution (1.5 mL, pH = 10.0), and subsequently, make up the volume with water to 3 mL. After incubation at 25 °C for 1 min, the emission spectra were measured (Ex = 365 nm).

We designed the 3D printing model of the portable testing equipment using CAD and used SLA light curing printing technology to print the equipment. We choose imported resin as printing material. The smartphone sensing platform is composed of a 3D print imaging model and a Huawei P30 Pro smartphone. Fluorescent photos are taken by the high-definition camera of the smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the BCDs and BSA-Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown by TEM, BSA-Cu NCs have good dispersion and nanoscale structure, indicating its successful synthesis (Fig. 1 A). HRTEM diagram (Fig. 1B) clearly shows the polycrystalline copper, characteristics, and the lattice stripe spacing is 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (111) crystal plane of metallic copper. The TEM diagram in Fig. 1C exhibits BCDs well monodispersed in solution. From the HRTEM diagram (Fig. 1D), we can see that the lattice fringe spacing is 0.234 nm. The above results indicate that BSA- Cu NCs and BCDs are of similar size, which is in good agreement with previously reported [41].

Based on fluorescence spectra and UV spectra, we analyzed the optical properties of BCDs and BSA-Cu NCs, respectively. When excited with 365 nm, the fluorescence emission peak and UV absorption peak of BCDs were at 420 nm and 340 nm, respectively. (Fig. 2 A). The fluorescence peak of BSA-Cu NCs was 635 nm and its UV absorption peak was 280 nm. (Fig. 2B). Moreover, the quantum yields (QY) of the as-prepared BCDs and BSA-Cu NCs were estimated to be 51.33 %, and 2.96 %, respectively (Fig. S1). Fig. 2C is the XRD of BCDs, exhibiting that the peak at around 2θ = 20.0° represents its (311) plane. Meanwhile, the organic molecule BSA was employed as the ligand for the chemical synthesis of Cu NCs. The surface functional groups of BSA-Cu NCs were identified by Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) to determine whether BSA was successfully incorporated into BSA-Cu NC. The infrared spectra of BSA alone and the as-prepared BSA-Cu NCs are displayed in Fig. 2D, respectively. In BSA, the peak near 1540 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1 attributes to –NH bending or –CN stretching vibrations, and the peak near 1650 cm−1 corresponds to amine scissoring [42]. These functional groups are related to the formation of BSA-Cu NCs. Similar peaks were seen in Cu NCs, indicating the successful synthesis of BSA-Cu NCs. Additionally, the peaks located at 3450 and 1650 cm−1 could be attributed to the vibration absorbance of OH and CO. BSA-directed Cu NCs have favorable biocompatibility and potential in biosensing applications [43]. According to the infrared spectrum of BCDs, the peak near 3450 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH. The peaks near 1640 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 come from the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO–, respectively [44]. In the spectra of BCDs and BSA-Cu NCs, it can be seen that there is no new characteristic band, which indicates that there is no interaction between them. The interaction between BCDs and BSA-Cu NCs was further demonstrated by zeta potential. As shown in Fig. S2, the zeta potential of BCDs is −22.2 mV, the zeta potential of BSA-Cu NCs is −11.3 mV, and the zeta of BCDs @BSA-Cu NCs is −15 mV, respectively. It can be inferred that BCDs and BSA are electrostatically repulsive and do not affect each other.

In addition, the surface chemical properties of BCDs and BSA-Cu NCs were measured by XPS. The full spectrum of BCDs shows four main peaks at 532 eV, 400 eV, 285 eV, and 164 eV, corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and S 2p, respectively (Fig. S3A). Fig. S3B shows that the peaks of 288.06 eV, 286.06 eV, and 284.56 eV originate from CO, CN, and CC/CC bonds, respectively [20]. Fig. S3C shows that the peaks of 400.16 eV and 401.46 eV are caused by CN and NH bonds respectively [45] Fig. S3D shows that the two peaks of 531.66 eV and 532.96 eV come from OC and OC bonds [39]. Fig. S3E shows that the two peaks of 163.26 eV and 164.56 eV are caused by CS 2p3/2 and CS 2p1/2, respectively [20]. The above results indicated the successful preparation of BCDs.

As shown in Fig. S4A, the full spectrum of BSA-Cu NCs shows that there are five main peaks at 933 eV, 532 eV, 400 eV, 285 eV, and 198 eV, corresponding to Cu 2p, O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and S 2p respectively. Fig. S4B shows that the peaks of 288.12 eV, 286.22 eV, and 284.82 eV originate from CO, CN, and CC/CC bonds, respectively. Fig. S4C shows that the peaks of 401.62 eV and 399.97 eV originate from CN and NH bonds respectively. Fig. S4D shows that the two peaks of 531.66 eV and 532.96 eV come from OC and OC bonds. Two peaks at 163.62, and 168.12 eV originate from S 2p3/2 of CS and CS (Fig. S4E) [39]. The binding energy position of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 were located at 932.67 eV and 952.52 eV, respectively (Fig. S4F) [20,46]. These results show that Cu NCs are mainly composed of Cu0 and Cu+, and Cu2+ is absent [46]. This is consistent with the previously reported Cu NCs.

We investigated the interaction between BCDs, BSA-Cu NCS, and TCs using 3D fluorescence spectroscopy. The 3D fluorescence spectra of BCDs, BSA-Cu NCS, and the mixed systems of BCDs, BSA-Cu NC, and TCs were measured. The emission peak of BCDs is at 420 nm (Fig. 3 A), and the emission peak of BSA-Cu NCs is at 635 nm (Fig. 3B). As shown in Fig. 3C, the mixture of BCDs and BSA-Cu NCs, It was found that the intensity at 420 nm decreased slightly, while the fluorescence at 635 nm was almost unchanged. When TCs was added to the mixed system, it was found that the fluorescence at 420 nm was quenched, and an emission peak rose at 520 nm, while the fluorescence at 635 nm remained relatively stable (Fig. 3D). The above results further indicate that there is little interaction between BCDs and BSA-Cu NCs, and the BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs sensing system has the feasibility to detect TCs.

By calculating BCDs, BSA-Cu NCs, and the fluorescence intensity ratio [I(635+520)/I420] at 520 nm, the important parameters such as pH, reaction time, and culture temperature were optimized to ensure the best experimental results. The optimum conditions were determined. BSA-Cu NCs are most sensitive to DC, but not so sensitive to other tetracycline antibiotics. Therefore, we mainly choose DC to optimize the experimental conditions. The ratiometric fluorescence intensity [I(635+520)/I420] of BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs solution and BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs/DC solution is the largest at pH = 10.0 (Fig. S5A). The ratiometric fluorescence intensity [I(635+520)/I420] of BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs solution and BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs/DC solution is the largest at 25 °C (Fig. S5B). BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs solution and BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs/DC solution remained stable after 1 min of reaction, and it can be seen that the reaction was completed at 1 min (Fig. S5C). Thus, the above results show that the optimal pH was 10.0, the reaction time was 1 min, and the culture temperature at 25 °C, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and visual sensing of TCs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs can be used as a selective fluorescence sensor for TCs series antibiotics. When the DC concentration increases, the blue fluorescence at 420 nm of BCDs is quenched and the green fluorescence peak rises, while the red fluorescence peak of BSA-Cu NCs remains basically unchanged. It is observed that with the increase in DC concentration, the fluorescence color of BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs varies from red to yellow to green. Similar phenomena were observed in TC, OTC, and CTC, indicating that the concentration range of TCs can be qualitatively estimated by the fluorescence of BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs with the naked eye under the ultraviolet lamp. Fig. 4 A shows the change of fluorescence curve with the increase of DC concentration, the fluorescence emission peak of the system at 520 nm gradually increased, the fluorescence emission intensity of BCDs gradually decreases, while the fluorescence peak of BSA-Cu NCs is basically unchanged. The I(635+520)/I420 and DC have an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.993) (Fig. 4B) in the range of 0.25–27 µM concentration, and the lower limit of detection (LOD) is 0.079 µM (3σ/N). Based on the fluorescence spectrum data, CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4C) for DC titration was made. With the addition of DC, the fluorescence color changed from red area to green area, which was consistent with the change of fluorescence visual color. The change of fluorescence curve with the increase of OTC concentration is shown in Fig. 4D. The I(635+520)/I420 and OTC have an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.998) (Fig. 4E) in the range of 0.32–30 µM concentration, and the LOD is 0.261 µM(3σ/N). Based on the fluorescence spectrum data, CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4F) for OTC titration was made. The change of fluorescence curve with the increase of TC concentration is shown in Fig. 4G. The I(635+520)/I420 and TC have an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.993) (Fig. 4H) in the range of 0.89–23 µM concentration, and the LOD is 0.417 µM (3σ/N). CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4I) for TC titration was made. The change of fluorescence curve with the increase of CTC concentration is shown in Fig. 4J. The I520/I635 and CTC have an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.995) (Fig. 4K) in the range of 1–24 µM concentration, and the LOD is 0.514 µM (3σ/N). CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4L) for TC titration was made. It can be seen that changes in fluorescence peaks at 420 nm and 520 nm can lead to color changes, which means that visual detection of tetracycline is feasible. Compared with other methods, although other methods have a wider detection range or lower detection limit, they do not have rich color changes and cannot achieve visual detection (Table S2). In short, the method proposed in this paper has the advantages of low cost, rich color changes, and convenience, and the determination is completed in about 1 min and is sufficient to monitor TCs at the upper limit in normal milk.

When TCs are added to the system, we found that the fluorescence at 635 nm is basically unchanged, the emission at 420 nm of BCDs is quenched, while the emission peak at 520 nm rises. To research the reaction mechanism, we measured the UV absorption spectrum of TCs and the fluorescence excitation and emission peak of BCDs, and found that the UV absorption peak of TCs overlapped with the excitation and emission peak of BCDs (Fig. 5 A). Through the fluorescence lifetime test (Fig. 5B), we found that the fluorescence lifetime has not changed significantly, Through the zeta potential test, it is found that the zeta potential of BCDs is −22.2 mV and the zeta potential of DC is −3.87 mV (Fig. S2) which shows that the fluorescence quenching of BCDs is due to the internal filtering effect, instead of resonant transfer of energy [47].

There is no fluorescence emission at 520 nm for a single BSA. When TCs are added, an obvious green fluorescence peak appears. Moreover, DC has a weak emission peak at 520 nm (Fig. 5C). This indicates that the green fluorescence peak after adding DC may come from DC. It can be found that when the same concentrations of DC, OTC, TC, and CTC are added to BSA, the emission peaks at 520 nm decrease in turn. Further, explore its mechanism. The sensitization of BSA to TCs is further verified. According to the literature, the three-dimensional structure of BSA contains a plurality of hydrophobic cavities [48], and TCs can enter the cavities and limit their rotation [49,50]. When TCs are in a hydrophobic environment, they have a higher quantum yield. Therefore, the sensitization of BSA to TCs may be the reason why BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs can be used as a probe for TCs. However, when adding CTC, we can see that not only does the emission peak at 520 nm rises, but also the emission peak at 420 nm rises significantly. According to the literature, the emission at 420 nm is also due to the interaction between CTC and amino acids in BSA, resulting in changes in the secondary structure of BSA [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT calculation of fluorescence detection mechanism of TCs
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted molecular docking for the combination mode of BSA and four antibiotics and used the Lamarckian genetic algorithm of the autodock Vina program for molecular docking. The structure file of the target protein is derived from the PDB database (pdb_id: 6qs9), the chain in 6qs9.pdb, and the PDB structure of the four antibiotics is obtained through chembio3d. Firstly, autodock tools software is used to add H atom, gasteiger-hücker empirical charge, combine non-polar hydrogen, and set rotatable keys to the structure files of the target and target small molecules. The receptor protein is completely regarded as a rigid structure. We selected the best binding conformation from the docking results for intermolecular interaction analysis and drew a three-dimensional interaction diagram through PyMOL software.

The binding energy of different antibiotics obtained by molecular docking is −8.0 kcal/mol for DC, −7.9 kcal/mol for CTC, and −7.5 kcal/mol for OTC and −7.4 kcal/mol for TC (Fig. 5D). The binding energy between DC and 6qs9 protein is −8.0 kcal/mol. DC forms hydrogen bonds with R217, K221, K294, and E291 (Fig. 6 A), and interacts with the residues within 4 Å of ligand by van der Waals force, including R194, R217, Q220, K221, E291, K294, P338, Y340, A341, V342, E443, P446, C447, D450, Y451 (Fig. 6B). The binding energy between CTC and 6qs9 protein is −7.9 kcal/mol. CTC forms hydrogen bonds with R217 and K221 (Fig. 6C) and interacts with the residues within 4 Å of ligand by van der Waals force, The residues within 4 Å of ligand are R194, R217, Q220, K221, E291, K294, P338, Y340, A341, V342, E443, P446, D450, and Y451 interact with each other by Van der Waals force (Fig. 6D). The binding energy of OTC with 6qs9 is −7.5 kcal/mol. OTC forms hydrogen bonds with R217, K221, A290, E291, K294, and P338 (Fig. 6E), and interacts with the residues within 4 Å of ligand by Van der Waals force, including R217, Q220, K221, A290, E291, V292, K294, E339, P338, V342, E443, P446 and D450 (Fig. 6F). The binding energy between TC and 6qs9 is −7.4 kcal/mol. TC forms a hydrogen bond with K294 (Fig. 6G) and interacts with the residues within ligand 4 Å as R194, R217, Q220, K221, E291, V292, E293, K294, Y340, A341, V342, P446, C447, D450 and Y451 by Van der Waals force (Fig. 6H). The binding energies of DC, OTC, and TC are consistent with our experimental results. However, CTC not only causes the fluorescence peak at 520 nm to rise but also the fluorescence peak at 420 nm. It is speculated that the binding between CTC and BSA is not only at 520 nm, but also the fluorescence peak at 420 nm is caused by the reaction between CTC and BSA. Therefore, the binding energy can't be seen from the emission peak at 520 nm alone. By comparing the binding energy, the binding energy of CTC is 7.9 kcal/mol, which also confirms that the fluorescence at 420 nm is also caused by the interaction with BSA, which is consistent with our experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective, anti-interference, stability and repeatability study
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the selectivity and anti-interference capability of the BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs, we compared the selectivity of the detection system for DC, OTC, TC, and CTC, and compared them with common interfering substances. It includes azithromycin, amoxicillin, streptomycin sulfate, erythromycin, glutathione, ascorbic acid, histidine, arginine, tryptophan, NaCl, FeCl3, AgCl, CdCl2, KCl, NH4Cl, DC, TC, OTC, CTC (Fig. S6A and Fig. S6B). We can see that BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs system has good selectivity for tetracycline antibiotics, and it is not difficult to see that DC has the best selectivity. In addition, we also tested the anti-interference ability of the system. As shown in Fig. S6C, Fig. S6D, and Fig. S6E, DC, OTC, TC, and various interfering substances were added to BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs, respectively, and then the fluorescence intensity of the system was measured. The I(635+520)/I420 values with DC, OTC, and TC increased sharply, and the I(635+520)/I420 value when only DC, OTC, or TC is contained and the value containing other interferents are not much different. CTC and interfering substances were added to BCDs/BSA-Cu NCs, and then the fluorescence intensity of the system was measured. The I520/I635 values with CTC increased sharply, and the I520/I635 value when only CTC is contained and the value containing other interferents are not much different (Fig. S6F). Therefore, it can be seen that the system has good anti-interference ability.

We tested the stability and repeatability of the fluorescent probe sensor by repeating 15 µM of DC, TC, OTC, and CTC (Fig.S7). The stability of tetracycline antibiotics has an acceptable RSD value. Besides, the repeatability of the sensor between batches was also studied. Each batch of sensors shows a similar response, and the repeatability between batches also has an acceptable RSD value, which indicates that the sensor has excellent repeatability.

A WeChat small program named “intelligent fluorescence analysis” was developed, which can quickly extract color information from fluorescent pictures. The applet uses the YOLO v3 target detection of deep learning (Fig. 7 A). In this work, we use the YOLO v3 algorithm to process the fluorescent image and extract the tube information in the image, and then combine it with the basic image color extraction algorithm to achieve the preparation and extraction of the tube color in the fluorescent image [52]. There are 111 pictures for the training model, and they are composed of pictures with different numbers, different colors, and different angles. They are divided into the training set and test set (Fig. S8). (Applet code link: https://github.com/tankWang1024/chemistry_chensuru_2022).

We used five parameters to evaluate the yolov3 model, including precision, recall, false positive rate (FPR), false negative rate (FNR) [53], and F1 [54]. The formula is as follows:
(1)
Recall =
TP
TP + FN
(2)
Precision =
TP
TP + FP
(3)
FNR = 1 - Recall =
FN
TP + FN
(4)
FPR = 1 - Precision =
FP
TP + FP
(5)
F1 =
2*Precision*Recall
Precision + Recall

The actual test tube detected is called TP (true positive). The number of times mistaken for a test tube is represented by FP (false positive), and the test tube not detected is represented by FN (false negative). The correct detection times of all test tubes are expressed by precision. The total number of true value tubes correctly detected is represented by the recall. The comprehensive performance of the whole training model is expressed by F1 [55]. Table S3 shows the specific values of each evaluation parameter. The results show that the model has good accuracy and can accurately identify test tubes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TCs detection based on smartphone portable and visual platform
ce:section.ce:para: After adding a certain amount of TCs to the BCDs /BSA-Cu NCs system, the fluorescent images are collected through 3D printing equipment (Fig. 7B) and smartphones, and then WeChat applet we developed is used to process uploaded images and establish the relationship between color information (RGB and HSV) and TCs concentration. The applet compares six different linear fitting methods, including Least Squares, Ridge region, Least absolute shrinkage and selection operator, Lasso Lars, Bayesian linear regression, and Support vector machine, to select the optimal algorithm for different detection objects.

Comparing different color channels, including RGB and HSV, we choose a more appropriate color channel and fitting algorithm for different detection objects. As shown in Fig. S9, we choose the G channel with the best linearity for DC. As shown in Fig. S10, we choose the G/B with the better linearity for OTC. As shown in Fig. S11, we choose the G channel with the best linearity for CTC. As shown in Fig. S12 we choose the G channel with the better linearity for TC. For the selection of algorithms, we use six fitting algorithms for DC (Fig. S13), OTC (Fig. S14), CTC (Fig. S15), and TC (Fig. S16) respectively for linear fitting. It is found that the five algorithms of Least squares, Ridge region, Least absolute shrinkage and selection operator, Lasso Lars, and Bayesian linear region have good effects, while the effect of the Support vector machine is not as good as the first five, so we choose best squares for the fitting method of TCs (Table S4 and Fig. S17). Fig. 7C shows the linearity of the WeChat applet of DC. When the DC concentration is in the range of 0–27 µM, there is a linear relationship between the G value and DC, and the linear equation is y = 4.5428x + 29.1078 (R2 = 0.993). Fig. 7D shows the linearity of the WeChat applet of OTC. When the OTC concentration is in the range of 0–30 µM, there is a linear relationship between G/B and OTC, and the linear equation is y = 0.0248x + 0.3902 (R2 = 0.9912). Fig. 7E shows the linearity of the WeChat applet of TC. When the TC concentration is in the range of 0–24 µM, there is a linear relationship between the G value and TC, and the linear equation is y = 2.8559x + 39.1132 (R2 = 0.9942). Fig. 7F shows the linearity of the WeChat applet of CTC. When the CTC concentration is in the range of 0–18 µM, there is a linear relationship between the G value and CTC, and the linear equation is y = 3.9609x + 62.9796 (R2 = 0.9875). Through parameter comparison, in general, compared with the other five algorithms, the least square method has the best effect in our system.

We determined the concentrations of TCs in milk and calculated the recovery of samples by the standard addition method (Table S5). The recovery of DC was calculated to be 98.78–101.87 %, the recovery of OTC was calculated to be 102.31–106.45 %, the recovery of TC was calculated to be 96.96–101.59 %, the recovery of CTC was calculated to be 100.40–101.56 %. In addition, we also determined the content of four tetracycline antibiotics in milk by using portable detection equipment and a smartphone detection platform (Table S6 and Fig. S18), and the DC recovery was calculated to be 101.87–104.03 %, OTC recovery was calculated to be 98.24–103.87 %, TC recovery was calculated to be 96.47–107.11 %, CTC recovery was calculated to be 99.34–109.49 %, respectively. Therefore, this method can accurately determine the level of tetracycline antibiotics in milk, which provides strong support for food safety monitoring.

In conclusion, we have developed a portable device integrating three emission fluorescence and a smartphone detection platform based on machine learning for the visual determination of TCs. Three emission fluorescent probes were prepared by simply mixing BCDs and BSA-CuNCs. When TCs was present, a new fluorescence emission peak appeared at 520 nm based on the sensitization of BSA to TCs, and with obvious color change, the color changed from red to yellow, and then to cyan. In addition, the interaction mode between BSA and TCs is studied by using density functional theory and experimental methods. The three emission fluorescence system based on machine learning equipment shows good stability, sensitivity, and specificity for the detection of TCs. The convenient detection of TCs residues shows good potential. The advantages of this work mainly include the following three points: (1) the structure of the trichromatic probe is very simple, which avoids the complex preparation process of BCDs and BSA-Cu NCs through the covalent bond formation. (2) The smartphone detection platform based on machine learning is very convenient and can achieve in situ real-time detection needs. (3) Importantly, visual detection by TCs provides a rich and wide range of color variations compared to ratio fluorescent probes reported previously in the literature. These show that our system has excellent performance against tetracycline antibiotics, and the rich color changes of the tricolor probe can achieve the effect of visual detection of tetracycline antibiotics. The smartphone detection platform based on machine learning also provides new possibilities for on-site real-time and rapid detection of antibiotics. In brief, the platform has certain practicability and provides help for the detection of antibiotic residues in food.
